security_audit.summary: All 54 static findings are false positives. The static scanner misinterprets markdown documentation code blocks as executable code, YAML frontmatter as crypto algorithms, and standard OpenTelemetry API calls as reconnaissance. This skill is legitimate DevOps documentation for distributed tracing with Jaeger and Tempo. No actual security risks identified.

content.user_title: Implement Distributed Tracing with Jaeger and Tempo
content.value_statement: Debugging microservices is complex when requests span multiple services. This skill provides code examples and configuration patterns to implement distributed tracing with Jaeger and Tempo for full visibility into request flows.
actual_capabilities: Set up Jaeger and Tempo in Kubernetes and Docker environments
Instrument Python, Node.js, and Go applications with OpenTelemetry
Configure context propagation across service boundaries
Implement sampling strategies for production environments
Analyze traces to identify latency bottlenecks and errors
Correlate logs with trace IDs for complete debugging
limitations: Does not provide pre-built applications or deployment infrastructure
Does not include managed Jaeger or Tempo services
Requires existing microservices architecture to trace
Does not cover vendor-specific tracing solutions

use_cases.title: SRE Debugging Production Issues
use_cases.description: Use trace IDs to follow requests across services and identify which microservice is causing latency or errors in production.

use_cases.title: DevOps Implementing Observability
use_cases.description: Add OpenTelemetry instrumentation to existing services and deploy Jaeger for centralized trace collection.

use_cases.title: Developer Understanding Dependencies
use_cases.description: Visualize service dependencies and understand request flow through the system architecture.

prompt_templates.title: Basic Jaeger Setup
prompt_templates.prompt: How do I deploy Jaeger using Docker Compose for local development?
prompt_templates.scenario: Getting started with Jaeger for the first time

prompt_templates.title: Python Instrumentation

prompt_templates.scenario: Adding tracing to Python web services

prompt_templates.title: Context Propagation
prompt_templates.prompt: How do I propagate trace context between microservices using HTTP headers?
prompt_templates.scenario: Ensuring traces continue across service boundaries

prompt_templates.title: Production Sampling
prompt_templates.prompt: What sampling strategy should I use in production to reduce tracing overhead while maintaining visibility?

output_examples.input: How do I set up Jaeger with Docker Compose?
output_examples.output:
Here is a basic Docker Compose configuration for Jaeger:

```yaml
version: '3.8'
services:
  jaeger:
    image: jaegertracing/all-in-one:latest
    ports:
      - "16686:16686"  # UI
      - "14268:14268"   # Collector
```

This starts the all-in-one Jaeger image with the UI on port 16686 and collector on port 14268. Access the UI at http://localhost:16686.

output_examples.input: How do I trace a Python Flask request?
output_examples.output:
Use OpenTelemetry to instrument your Flask application. First install the packages: pip install opentelemetry-api opentelemetry-sdk opentelemetry-exporter-jaeger opentelemetry-instrumentation-flask

Then add the initialization code to create a tracer provider and instrument Flask. The spans will automatically be sent to your Jaeger collector.

best_practices: Start with head-based sampling at 1-10% then adjust based on traffic volume
Add meaningful span attributes like user_id and request_id for filtering
Propagate trace context in all HTTP headers between services
anti_patterns: Do not sample 100% of traces in production as it creates excessive overhead
Avoid creating spans for every function call; focus on external calls and database operations
Do not hardcode collector endpoints; use environment variables for configuration

faq.question: What is the difference between Jaeger and Tempo?
faq.answer: Jaeger is a full-stack tracing solution with its own storage. Tempo is Grafana's distributed tracing backend that uses object storage like S3 for cost-effective long-term trace retention.

faq.question: How much overhead does distributed tracing add?
faq.answer: Properly configured tracing adds less than 1% CPU overhead. Use batch span processors and appropriate sampling rates to minimize impact.

faq.question: Can I use this skill with AWS X-Ray?
faq.answer: This skill focuses on Jaeger and Tempo. For AWS X-Ray, you would use the AWS X-Ray SDKs instead of OpenTelemetry exporters.

faq.question: What programming languages are supported?
faq.answer: The skill includes examples for Python, Node.js, and Go. OpenTelemetry supports many more languages including Java, .NET, and Ruby.

faq.question: How do I correlate logs with traces?
faq.answer: Extract the trace_id from the current span and include it in your log statements. This allows you to filter logs by trace ID in your logging system.

faq.question: Do I need to modify every service for tracing to work?
faq.answer: Traces will only show spans from instrumented services. However, context propagation allows traces to continue even through uninstrumented services if the headers are forwarded.
